ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for murtabak specifically; FOODON_03315595 (stuffed flatbread food product) is the closest defensible parent class. Variant-specific URI not available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Pancakes and similar products) is the closest defensible category for murtabak, a pan-fried stuffed dough product. No murtabak-specific FoodEx2 code exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for murtabak or stuffed Indian pancake found in published SNOMED CT release as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of murtabak is approximately 150 g, representing roughly half of a standard whole murtabak as sold at hawker stalls and Indian Muslim restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Murtabak (chicken, mushroom, cheese) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Murtabak (chicken, mushroom, cheese), CR ID F05020065. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are used as-is. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous stuffed flatbread data (see notes) as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for murtabak with chicken, mushroom and cheese exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: stuffed wheat-flour flatbread / paratha with protein filling, which has a reported GI of approximately 62 in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The high protein content (21.9 g/100g) and moderate fat (19.4 g/100g) are expected to attenuate the glycemic response relative to plain wheat flatbread (GI ~70). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (62 × 8.92 g carbohydrate × 1.5) / 100 = 8.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 358 mg/100g; cheese and seasoning contribute cumulative sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as curry dipping sauce, as the combined sodium intake can approach or exceed the 600 mg per-meal threshold recommended for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (8.92 g/100g) and high protein (21.9 g/100g) attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content and high protein density make this a relatively low glycemic-load option for people with type 2 diabetes; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 13.4 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of 8.3, which is within acceptable limits when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or sweetened curry sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~7.2 g/100g and trans fat ~0.1 g/100g from pan-frying oil and cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should moderate intake due to the estimated saturated fat content of approximately 7.2 g/100g, which exceeds the 5 g/100g threshold for a high-saturated-fat food; requesting preparation with minimal oil and reduced cheese can lower the saturated fat contribution meaningfully.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~7.2 g/100g exceeds 5 g/100g threshold; pan-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); trace trans fat 0.1 g/100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume this food sparingly, as the combination of high saturated fat from cheese and cooking oil, AGE formation from pan-frying, and trace trans fat creates a pro-inflammatory nutritional profile that may exacerbate joint inflammation; mushrooms in the filling provide some anti-inflammatory beta-glucan benefit but do not offset the overall concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murtabak with chicken, mushroom and cheese is a premium variant of the classic South Asian and Southeast Asian stuffed flatbread, widely sold at Indian Muslim hawker stalls and restaurants across Singapore. The dish consists of a thin, unleavened wheat-flour dough that is stretched and folded around a savoury filling of spiced chicken (often tandoori-style), sliced mushrooms, and melted cheese, then pan-fried in oil on a flat griddle until the exterior is golden and crisp while the interior remains moist and flavourful. The result is a rich, layered pancake with a slightly chewy dough exterior, a juicy protein-dense filling, and pockets of melted cheese that add creaminess and depth. Nutritionally, this variant is notable for its high protein content of 21.9 g per 100 g, moderate fat of 19.4 g per 100 g (with an estimated saturated fat of approximately 7.2 g per 100 g from cheese and cooking oil), and a relatively low carbohydrate content of 8.92 g per 100 g, yielding 298 kcal per 100 g. Sodium is 358 mg per 100 g, placing it in the borderline-moderate range for sodium-sensitive individuals. The glycemic index is estimated at 62 (medium), and a standard 150 g serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 8.3, making it a comparatively low glycemic-load meal option. Clinically, the high protein and low carbohydrate profile make it suitable for people with type 2 diabetes in moderate portions, while the saturated fat and sodium content require caution for those managing cardiovascular disease or hypertension. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker contexts and is typically served with a curry dipping sauce that adds additional sodium. It is commonly consumed as a main meal, breakfast, or substantial snack.
common_user_queries: How many calories in murtabak chicken mushroom cheese?
Is murtabak good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05020065), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous data and clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database with documented basis. All mandatory multilingual names are present.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (7.2 g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central data for analogous stuffed wheat-flour flatbread with meat and cheese filling (USDA FDC ID 782166, 'Stuffed flatbread with meat and cheese'), as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. This estimate should be replaced with a direct HPB or ICMR-NIN value if one becomes available. 2. Trans fat (0.1 g/100g) is carried forward from the input record's health_suitability section as an estimated value consistent with pan-frying in commercial cooking oil; no direct source measurement is available. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient, fatty acid profile, and moisture data for this specific murtabak variant are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05020065) or the USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. The dietary_tags in the input included 'vegetarian', which is inconsistent with the chicken filling; this tag has been removed. The halal and high_protein tags are retained. 5. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this specific murtabak variant does not exist in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (62) × carbohydrate per serving (8.92 g/100g × 150 g / 100) / 100 = 62 × 13.38 / 100 = 8.3.
